text: The surgeon performs sharp adhesiolysis using scissors to carefully separate dense adhesions between the small intestine and the anterior abdominal wall. The small intestine is visibly adhered to the previous laparotomy scar, requiring precise dissection to avoid damage.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to safely separate dense peritoneal bands from the small intestine, minimizing the risk of damage during adhesiolysis.

The surgeon frees a band attached to the small intestine, releasing one loop of intestine while multiple other loops remain adhered. The serosa of the intestine is visibly attached to surrounding structures.

The surgeon uses sharp dissection to separate two loops of intestine that are adhered, potentially causing severe obstruction and internal herniation. An atraumatic clasp is used to lift one of the intestine loops, allowing gravity to assist in finding a dissection plane. The loops are successfully separated, revealing the pelvis adhered to the small intestine and sigmoid colon.

The surgeon performs blunt dissection to separate the sigmoid colon from the large endometrioma, revealing the ovary beneath. Both ovaries are closely positioned, forming a tubovarian mass with the sigmoid colon and omentum. The surgeon mobilizes the sigmoid colon to delineate the ovary's boundaries, carefully separating the attached bands and preserving the marginal artery of the sigmoid colon. The ovary is visible with a pearly white appearance as it is separated from the sigmoid colon.

A needle is used to puncture the ovary, releasing chocolate-colored material from the endometrioma. The harmonic device cuts the ovary for de-roofing, and suction is applied to drain the fluid from the cyst. The cystic wall is visible, and an atraumatic grasper is used to hold and elevate the cyst. Blunt dissection separates the cyst from the ovary, with suction irrigation removing the leaking endometrioma fluid.

The surgeon continues the cystectomy, removing the cyst from the ovary. The cyst is densely adhered at the base, requiring repositioning of the chromatic grasper to separate the wall. Harmonic is used to cut and perform marsupialization, leaving the cystic wall over the anterior cul-de-sac for later removal. The remnant of the cyst inside the ovary is separated and fragmented using harmonic, which also fulgurates the endometrioma remnant to prevent recurrence. The margin of the ovary is burned, and the ovarian cystic wall is repeatedly removed. A drain is introduced into the ovary pocket, followed by thorough lavage with nine liters of Ringer lactate to clean the area.

The surgeon separates the intestinal loops and performs suction to remove fluid from the ovarian pocket. Irrigation is initiated, shaking the intestines to dislodge remnant endometrial tissue, followed by further suction. Approximately one liter of ringer lactate is left inside the abdomen to prevent re-adhesion. The tip of a Foley catheter is visible, and the fluid color becomes clear after irrigation.
